reason: Within an ≥80 cohort, incremental age still correlates with reduced renal reserve and higher susceptibility to perioperative ischemic/nephrotoxic AKI, though effect size may be modest due to restricted range.

Sex has inconsistent and generally modest associations with post-cardiac surgery AKI compared with hemodynamics and baseline kidney disease; it is mainly a proxy for body size and comorbidity patterns.
Race category is not a direct physiologic driver of AKI; any association is largely mediated by comorbidities, access, and baseline CKD rather than perioperative renal perfusion.
Black race can correlate with higher baseline CKD burden and AKI risk in some cohorts, but it is an indirect proxy rather than a mechanistic perioperative predictor.
Race is not a direct biomarker of renal injury; observed differences are typically confounded by comorbidity and baseline renal function.
May proxy higher prevalence of diabetes/CKD in some populations, but it is not a direct perioperative determinant of creatinine rise.
Potential indirect association via metabolic comorbidity burden, but limited direct mechanistic linkage to postoperative AKI.
Heterogeneous category with weak interpretability; any relationship to AKI is indirect and confounded.
Ethnicity is not a direct physiologic predictor of postoperative creatinine rise; associations are typically mediated by comorbidities and baseline kidney health.
Height has minimal direct relationship to renal perfusion or tubular injury; it mainly contributes to body size indexing and dosing, which are better captured by weight/BSA.

Higher weight can reflect obesity and altered hemodynamics/drug dosing and is associated with AKI risk in some surgical cohorts, but it is an indirect predictor compared with perfusion and nephrotoxins.
Family history does not meaningfully inform acute postoperative renal perfusion, nephrotoxin exposure, or tubular injury risk.
Diabetes is an established risk factor for postoperative AKI due to microvascular disease and reduced renal reserve, especially relevant in cardiac surgery.
Poor glycemic control can worsen endothelial dysfunction and infection risk and may correlate with CKD, but it is a less direct predictor than diabetes presence and perioperative hemodynamics.
Dyslipidemia is not a direct driver of perioperative AKI; it is a general cardiovascular risk marker with weak incremental renal prognostic value.
Hypertension is associated with chronic nephrosclerosis and reduced renal autoregulatory reserve, increasing vulnerability to perioperative hypotension and AKI.

Prior endocarditis can imply chronic inflammation, embolic risk, and more complex surgery, which may indirectly increase AKI risk. It is not a primary physiologic driver of postoperative creatinine rise compared with hemodynamics and nephrotoxins.

COPD is associated with perioperative hypoxemia/hypercapnia and higher risk of low cardiac output and vasopressor exposure, which can impair renal perfusion. It is an established but indirect AKI risk factor.

Home oxygen use is a marker of severe chronic lung disease and limited cardiopulmonary reserve, increasing risk of perioperative hypoxia and hemodynamic instability that can precipitate AKI. It is a severity proxy rather than a direct renal biomarker.

Bronchodilator use mainly reflects underlying obstructive lung disease and perioperative respiratory management. It has only an indirect association with AKI through illness severity and hypoxemia risk.

Sleep apnea is linked to chronic intermittent hypoxia, sympathetic activation, and cardiometabolic disease, which can increase perioperative complications. Its relationship to postoperative AKI is plausible but not strongly direct.

Active/recent pneumonia increases systemic inflammation, sepsis risk, hypoxemia, and likelihood of vasopressors/ventilation, all of which can reduce renal perfusion and raise AKI risk. This is a meaningful perioperative risk factor.

IV drug use can correlate with infections, hepatitis, and social/medical complexity, which may indirectly increase perioperative risk. It is not an established direct predictor of postoperative AKI in cardiac surgery.

Depression has minimal direct physiologic linkage to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity in the immediate postoperative period. Any association is likely confounded by comorbidity burden and care patterns.
Alcohol use can contribute to liver disease, malnutrition, and hemodynamic instability, which may indirectly affect AKI risk. By itself it is a weak and nonspecific predictor.

Liver disease is associated with altered hemodynamics (vasodilation, low effective arterial volume), coagulopathy, and higher risk of perioperative hypotension and infection, all increasing AKI susceptibility. It is an established comorbidity risk factor.

Immunosuppression increases infection/sepsis risk and may involve nephrotoxic agents (e.g., calcineurin inhibitors), both relevant to AKI. It is a moderate, indirect predictor without being a direct renal marker.

Prior mediastinal radiation can indicate complex cardiac pathology and technically challenging surgery with higher bypass time/bleeding risk, indirectly raising AKI risk. The link is plausible but not primary.

Cancer can reflect frailty, inflammation, and exposure to nephrotoxic chemotherapy/contrast, which may increase AKI risk. However, it is heterogeneous and generally an indirect predictor in this setting.

PAD is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and microvascular disease, associated with baseline CKD risk and impaired renal perfusion reserve. It is a well-established comorbidity increasing postoperative AKI risk.
Thoracic aortic disease often implies complex surgery with longer cross-clamp/CPB time and higher embolic/low-flow risk, which can precipitate AKI. It is an established procedural-risk marker.

Syncope is nonspecific and may reflect arrhythmia or valvular disease, but it does not directly indicate renal vulnerability. Its association with AKI is weak and largely mediated by underlying cardiac pathology captured elsewhere.

History of unresponsiveness is an indirect marker of frailty/neurologic comorbidity and overall illness severity, which can correlate with postoperative complications. It has no direct mechanistic link to renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity.

Cerebrovascular disease reflects systemic atherosclerosis and vascular stiffness, which can accompany renal microvascular disease and higher perioperative risk. It is an indirect rather than primary predictor of postoperative AKI.

Preoperative anemia reduces oxygen delivery and increases transfusion likelihood, both associated with cardiac-surgery–associated AKI. It is a common, established perioperative risk factor but not a direct kidney biomarker.
Low pre-op albumin indicates malnutrition/inflammation and lower oncotic pressure, associated with worse surgical outcomes and AKI risk. It is an established but nonspecific risk marker.

Elevated bilirubin suggests hepatic dysfunction/congestion and higher perioperative risk, often correlating with venous congestion and low effective renal perfusion. It is an indirect predictor of AKI in cardiac surgery patients.

Higher HbA1c reflects diabetes/chronic glycemic exposure, which is strongly associated with underlying CKD and susceptibility to ischemic/nephrotoxic injury. It is an established comorbidity-level risk factor for postoperative AKI.
HIT antibodies mainly affect anticoagulation strategy and thrombotic risk; any renal impact is indirect and uncommon. Not a typical driver of creatinine rise after cardiac surgery.

Higher MELD indicates significant hepatic dysfunction and systemic illness, often with hemodynamic instability and venous congestion that predispose to AKI. It is a strong global risk score in major surgery populations.

Elevated BNP reflects heart failure and volume/pressure overload, which can cause renal venous congestion and low forward flow (cardiorenal physiology). It is a strong predictor of postoperative renal dysfunction in cardiac surgery.
Poor 5-meter walk performance is a frailty marker associated with worse postoperative outcomes and complications. Its relationship to AKI is indirect via overall physiologic reserve.

Prior cardiovascular interventions indicate advanced coronary/vascular disease and higher operative complexity, which can increase AKI risk indirectly. It is not a direct physiologic marker of renal injury.
Redo/previous CABG often implies more complex surgery and higher risk of low cardiac output or longer bypass time, both linked to AKI. It is an established but indirect risk factor.

Prior cardiac surgery suggests redo sternotomy/greater operative complexity and potential for hemodynamic instability, increasing AKI risk. The association is indirect and mediated by perioperative course.
Prior PCI indicates coronary disease burden and may correlate with comorbid CKD, but it does not directly affect postoperative renal perfusion. Overall an indirect risk marker.

PCI during the same episode often implies recent contrast exposure and acute coronary syndrome physiology, both increasing susceptibility to postoperative AKI. This is a more proximate and established risk factor than remote PCI.
Presence of a stent reflects coronary disease history and may influence perioperative antiplatelet management, but renal effects are indirect. Not a primary predictor of creatinine rise.

Short interval since prior intervention can proxy higher baseline cardiovascular disease burden and procedural complexity, which indirectly increases AKI risk. It is not a direct physiologic driver of postoperative creatinine rise.

History of prior cardiac interventions suggests advanced atherosclerosis/heart failure and potentially more complex redo surgery, both associated with higher AKI risk. The link is indirect and mediated through hemodynamics and operative factors.

ACE inhibitors can reduce efferent arteriolar tone and impair renal autoregulation during perioperative hypotension/vasoplegia, increasing AKI susceptibility. Effect size is moderate and context-dependent (volume status, shock).
Recent P2Y12 inhibitor use mainly increases bleeding/transfusion risk, which can secondarily contribute to AKI via hypotension and inflammation. It is not a primary renal insult marker.
Amiodarone use is a marker of arrhythmia burden and cardiac disease severity, which may correlate with perioperative instability. It has no strong direct nephrotoxic effect in this setting.

Pre-op anticoagulation can increase bleeding and transfusion needs, indirectly raising AKI risk through hemodynamic compromise and inflammatory load. Direct renal injury is uncommon except in specific scenarios (e.g., anticoagulant-related nephropathy).
Aspirin at typical antiplatelet doses has minimal direct nephrotoxicity and is common standard-of-care in cardiac patients. Any AKI association is weak and largely confounded by comorbidity.
Beta-blockers may influence perioperative hemodynamics (bradycardia/low output) but are generally protective and routine. Association with AKI is indirect and inconsistent.
Chronic beta-blocker therapy mainly reflects baseline cardiac management and is not a direct renal risk factor. Any AKI signal is likely confounding by underlying cardiac disease.
Chronic calcium channel blocker use has no strong mechanistic link to postoperative AKI and is common in older patients. It may weakly proxy hypertension but is not a primary predictor.
GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor use suggests recent acute coronary syndrome/PCI and higher bleeding/transfusion risk, which can contribute to AKI indirectly. Not a direct renal biomarker.
Need for pre-op inotropes indicates low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology, strongly linked to renal hypoperfusion and subsequent AKI. This is a direct marker of hemodynamic vulnerability.
Statin/lipid-lowering therapy is common and not a direct driver of AKI; if anything, statins may be mildly protective. Any association is weak and confounded by vascular disease.
Chronic nitrate therapy indicates significant ischemic heart disease and potential hemodynamic fragility, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Nitrates themselves are not nephrotoxic.

IV nitrates pre-op suggest active ischemia/heart failure and can contribute to perioperative hypotension, which may reduce renal perfusion. This is a moderate, indirect-to-semidirect hemodynamic risk marker.
Other antianginal therapy reflects coronary disease severity and potential perioperative instability, indirectly related to AKI. It is not a direct renal injury mechanism.

Chronic or recent steroids can reflect frailty/inflammation and may worsen glycemic control/infection risk, indirectly affecting AKI risk. Not a direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxin signal in most cardiac surgery patients.
Primarily influences perioperative bleeding/transfusion needs rather than kidney injury directly. Any AKI association is indirect via hemodynamic instability or transfusion exposure.

Warfarin use is mainly a marker of atrial fibrillation/valvular disease and bleeding risk; it is not a typical direct driver of postoperative AKI. Potential effects are indirect through bleeding, hypotension, or transfusion.
Similar to other anticoagulants, it mainly affects bleeding risk and perioperative management rather than renal perfusion directly. Any relationship to AKI is indirect.
Acts as an anticoagulation exposure with indirect links to AKI through bleeding/transfusion or perioperative instability. Not an established independent predictor of postoperative creatinine rise.

Statin use may be a marker of atherosclerotic burden and has mixed evidence for renal protection vs no effect in cardiac surgery. Overall a weak-to-plausible indirect predictor rather than a direct AKI driver.

Recent catheterization implies contrast exposure and higher coronary disease burden, both associated with postoperative AKI risk. Particularly relevant in older adults where contrast-associated nephropathy and limited renal reserve are concerns.

Known coronary anatomy disease reflects systemic atherosclerosis and higher surgical complexity, which can increase AKI risk indirectly. It is less specific than measures of hemodynamic insult or renal function.

Greater coronary disease burden correlates with more complex surgery and higher likelihood of low cardiac output states, which can reduce renal perfusion. Still an indirect predictor compared with direct hemodynamic/lab markers.

Severe aortic stenosis/regurgitation can predispose to perioperative hemodynamic instability and low forward flow, increasing AKI risk. Association is moderate and mediated through cardiac output and perfusion.

Mitral disease can be associated with pulmonary hypertension, low output, and congestion, which can impair renal perfusion and venous drainage. Typically an indirect but established risk context for postoperative AKI.

Tricuspid disease often reflects right-sided failure and elevated venous pressures, which are strongly linked to renal congestion and AKI risk. This can be a meaningful predictor in postoperative cardiac surgery patients.
Pulmonic valve disease is less common and usually less directly tied to renal hypoperfusion than left-sided or tricuspid pathology. Any AKI link is generally indirect via right heart dysfunction.

Redo sternotomy/cardiac surgery increases operative complexity, bypass time, bleeding/transfusion, and risk of low cardiac output—all contributors to AKI. A reasonably established preoperative risk factor.

If this encodes operative urgency or baseline clinical status, it can correlate with physiologic reserve and perioperative instability affecting AKI risk. Without clear definition it remains a moderate, nonspecific predictor.
Case cancellation is an administrative/operational outcome and does not represent a physiologic mechanism for postoperative creatinine rise. It is unlikely to add meaningful predictive value for AKI.
Conversion (e.g., minimally invasive to open) is a proxy for operative complexity, longer ischemic/CPB time, bleeding, and hemodynamic instability, all of which increase AKI risk.

Robotic approach may correlate with less surgical trauma and shorter recovery, but it is not a direct physiologic driver of renal injury and effects are largely mediated by other variables (bleeding, hypotension, CPB time).
CABG is associated with CPB exposure (if on-pump), atheroembolism risk, and perioperative hemodynamic swings that can precipitate AKI, especially in very elderly patients.
Valve surgery often entails longer CPB/cross-clamp times and higher risk of low cardiac output syndrome, both strongly linked to postoperative renal hypoperfusion and AKI.
Additional cardiac procedures generally indicate greater operative complexity and longer bypass/ischemia time, increasing inflammatory load and risk of renal hypoperfusion.
Concomitant AF procedures (e.g., Maze) add operative time and complexity but are not independently strong renal insults beyond their correlation with longer surgery/CPB.
Aortic procedures carry high AKI risk due to prolonged CPB, hypothermia/circulatory arrest, and embolic/ischemic renal injury mechanisms.
Non-cardiac concomitant procedures can increase operative duration and physiologic stress, but renal impact is indirect and heterogeneous.
Appropriate prophylactic antibiotic choice mainly affects infection risk; it has only a weak, indirect relationship to AKI unless nephrotoxic agents are specifically implicated.
Timing of prophylactic antibiotics is primarily a quality metric for surgical site infection prevention and has no meaningful direct link to postoperative creatinine rise.
Discontinuation ordering reflects protocol adherence and does not directly affect renal perfusion or tubular injury in the early postoperative window.
An extra dose is usually driven by case length/blood loss; any AKI association is indirect via operative complexity rather than antibiotic exposure itself.
Lower nadir temperature can reflect deeper hypothermia and more complex CPB strategies (including circulatory arrest), which are associated with renal ischemia/inflammation and AKI risk.
Low intraoperative hemoglobin indicates anemia/hemodilution/bleeding and reduced oxygen delivery, which is a strong contributor to renal medullary hypoxia and postoperative AKI.
Intraoperative hyperglycemia is associated with stress response and worse outcomes, but its relationship to AKI is indirect and confounded by illness severity and diabetes.
Use of specialized coronary perfusion techniques is a marker of complex myocardial protection/ischemia risk; AKI linkage is indirect through operative complexity and hemodynamic instability.
Aortic cannulation is common and usually reflects standard CPB setup rather than renal-specific risk. Any AKI signal is indirect via overall case complexity and embolic/atheromatous burden.

Femoral cannulation often indicates peripheral vascular disease, redo/emergent cases, or complex aortic pathology, which correlate with higher AKI risk. It may also increase embolic risk and malperfusion potential indirectly affecting renal perfusion.

Axillary cannulation is typically chosen for complex aortic surgery or severe atherosclerosis, which can associate with AKI through longer/complex CPB and embolic risk. The cannulation site itself is not a direct renal insult.

Innominate cannulation is used in complex arch/aortic procedures and can be a proxy for higher operative complexity and circulatory management that increases AKI risk. The relationship is indirect rather than mechanistic.
Other arterial sites mainly act as a nonspecific marker of unusual anatomy or complex surgery. Without context, it has limited direct linkage to renal injury.
Femoral venous cannulation can indicate minimally invasive/redo/complex access or hemodynamic instability, indirectly associated with AKI risk. It is not a direct driver of renal tubular injury.
Jugular venous cannulation for CPB is relatively uncommon and mostly reflects operative approach. Any AKI association is indirect via case complexity rather than renal physiology.

Right atrial venous cannulation is standard for many CPB cases and provides little incremental information about renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxin exposure. Thus it has weak prognostic value for creatinine rise.
Left atrial cannulation is typically used in specific complex procedures and may correlate with longer bypass time and hemodynamic perturbations that increase AKI risk. The link remains indirect.
Pulmonary venous/arterial cannulation is procedure-specific and mainly a proxy for surgical complexity. It has no direct renal biomarker relationship.
Bicaval cannulation is common in valve/congenital-type exposures and can be associated with longer CPB and more transfusion/hemolysis, which can contribute to AKI. Still, it is an indirect marker.
Other venous sites are nonspecific and mostly reflect unusual operative approach or anatomy. Any association with AKI is likely mediated by overall complexity rather than direct renal effects.

Longer CPB time is a strong, well-established predictor of postoperative AKI via inflammation, hemolysis, nonpulsatile flow, and renal hypoperfusion. It is one of the most informative intraoperative risk markers for subsequent creatinine rise.

Circulatory arrest implies profound systemic hypoperfusion and high-complexity aortic surgery, both strongly linked to AKI. Even with protective strategies, it is a major risk factor for renal ischemia-reperfusion injury.

Longer DHCA time increases duration of global low-flow/ischemia and reperfusion stress, strongly elevating AKI risk. It captures severity/duration of the insult more directly than the binary arrest indicator.

Use of cerebral perfusion is primarily a marker of arch surgery complexity and circulatory management rather than a direct renal determinant. It may correlate with longer CPB/arrest times, but adds limited independent renal-specific signal.

Longer selective cerebral perfusion often co-occurs with complex arch surgery, longer bypass, and systemic hypoperfusion, which can increase AKI risk. It is an indirect proxy rather than a kidney-specific exposure.

Circulatory arrest implies profound systemic ischemia/low-flow time and typically longer CPB/complex surgery, strongly linked to postoperative AKI. Longer arrest increases risk of renal ischemia-reperfusion injury.

Aortic occlusion is common in cardiac surgery and is not itself a direct renal insult unless it reflects more complex/hemodynamically stressful procedures. Its association with AKI is mostly indirect via operative complexity and perfusion changes.

Longer cross-clamp time correlates with longer cardiopulmonary bypass and ischemia-reperfusion/inflammatory burden, a well-established driver of CSA-AKI. It is a strong intraoperative predictor though not a direct renal biomarker.
Use of cerebral oximetry reflects monitoring practice and case complexity rather than a direct physiologic mechanism for AKI. It may weakly proxy higher-acuity surgery but is not kidney-specific.

Diffuse aortic calcification indicates advanced atherosclerosis and higher risk of embolic events and complex operative strategy, which can worsen renal perfusion and AKI risk. It is an established comorbidity-level risk marker.
Whether the aorta/arch was assessed is largely a procedural/behavioral marker and not a direct determinant of renal injury. It may weakly reflect surgical complexity.
Plaque assessment is primarily a documentation/monitoring variable; it does not directly change renal physiology. Any AKI association would be indirect via case complexity.

An altered plan due to aortic condition suggests unexpected complexity (e.g., arch work, longer CPB, embolic risk) and potential hemodynamic instability, which increases AKI risk. It is a meaningful proxy for higher-risk intraoperative course.
Refusal of blood products is not a physiologic exposure and is uncommon; its relationship to AKI is not established. Any effect would be highly confounded and indirect.

Receiving blood products reflects bleeding/complex surgery and transfusion-related inflammation/volume shifts, which are associated with CSA-AKI. It is a moderate-to-strong proxy but nonspecific across product types.

RBC transfusion is consistently associated with postoperative AKI via severity of bleeding, hemodilution/oxygen delivery issues, and transfusion-related inflammation. Dose-response relationships are commonly observed in cardiac surgery cohorts.
FFP transfusion indicates coagulopathy/bleeding and greater operative stress, which correlates with AKI risk. The product itself is less directly linked than RBCs, making it a moderate proxy.

Platelet transfusion suggests significant bleeding or platelet dysfunction (often with prolonged CPB), both associated with AKI risk. It is a moderate indirect predictor rather than a direct renal insult.

Cryoprecipitate use reflects severe bleeding/hypofibrinogenemia and complex surgery, which can accompany hypotension and inflammation contributing to AKI. Evidence is more indirect and less consistent than for RBC transfusion.

Abnormal intraoperative clotting factor status (or factor administration) is a marker of coagulopathy/bleeding severity and operative complexity, indirectly associated with AKI. It is not a direct renal biomarker and is likely confounded by transfusion and CPB duration.
Antifibrinolytic use is more a marker of bleeding risk/complexity than a direct renal insult. Only indirect AKI linkage via transfusion/hemorrhagic shock pathways.
Similar to aminocaproic acid, it is not typically nephrotoxic and mainly reflects bleeding-risk management. Any AKI association is indirect through surgical complexity and hemodynamic instability.
TEE performance is routine in many cardiac surgeries and does not directly affect renal perfusion or nephrotoxin exposure. It is a weak proxy for case type rather than AKI biology.

Residual aortic regurgitation can reduce effective forward flow and contribute to low diastolic pressure, impairing renal perfusion. AKI risk is moderate via hemodynamic consequences rather than direct renal injury.
Residual MR can cause low cardiac output and elevated left-sided pressures, worsening renal congestion/perfusion mismatch. This is an established hemodynamic risk factor for postoperative AKI.

Residual TR strongly relates to venous congestion and elevated CVP, which is a well-supported driver of cardiorenal syndrome and postoperative AKI. Particularly relevant in older patients with limited renal reserve.
Valve surgery often implies longer bypass/cross-clamp time and higher hemodynamic complexity, increasing AKI risk. It is an established but nonspecific procedural risk marker.
Concomitant AF procedures (e.g., Maze) mainly indicate added operative complexity and time, with only indirect AKI linkage. Not a primary physiologic driver of renal injury.
General anesthesia is standard for cardiac surgery and provides little discriminatory information for AKI risk in this cohort. Any renal effects are mediated by hemodynamics captured elsewhere.

PCC use suggests significant bleeding/coagulopathy and potential shock/transfusion exposure, which can contribute to AKI indirectly. PCC itself is not a classic nephrotoxin, so the signal is mainly severity-related.

Higher residual gradient may reflect prosthesis mismatch or obstruction that can limit cardiac output, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. It is a plausible but not definitive predictor compared with direct hemodynamic/lab markers.

Paravalvular leak can cause ongoing regurgitation and reduced effective forward flow, predisposing to low-output states and renal hypoperfusion. It also signals procedural complications that increase AKI risk.

Elevated mitral gradient can indicate stenosis/prosthesis mismatch leading to pulmonary hypertension and reduced forward output, indirectly impacting kidneys. Association is plausible but typically weaker than overt regurgitation or RV failure.
Mitral PVL can drive persistent volume overload and low effective cardiac output, increasing risk of renal hypoperfusion and congestion. Also reflects procedural complication burden linked to AKI.

Combined significant MR/TR implies biventricular volume overload with both low forward flow and venous congestion, a strong setup for cardiorenal AKI. This pattern often correlates with difficult postoperative hemodynamics.

Lower post-procedure EF is a strong indicator of reduced cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk after cardiac surgery. It is a clinically meaningful predictor of subsequent creatinine rise, especially in the elderly.

Planned PCI mainly reflects coronary disease complexity and potential contrast exposure, but by itself is an indirect and inconsistent driver of postoperative AKI compared with hemodynamics and renal labs.
Need for an intra-aortic balloon pump is a strong marker of low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock risk, which directly threatens renal perfusion and is strongly associated with postoperative AKI.
Baseline cath-assist/support suggests higher preoperative cardiac instability and procedural complexity, which can increase AKI risk indirectly, but it is not a direct renal insult or biomarker.

ECMO indicates severe cardiopulmonary failure with profound hemodynamic instability, inflammation/hemolysis, and high vasopressor exposure—conditions strongly linked to subsequent AKI and need for RRT.
Blood product transfusion/requirement is associated with bleeding, inflammation, hemolysis, and hypotension; higher transfusion burden after cardiac surgery is a strong predictor of AKI.
Extubation in the OR is a proxy for lower illness severity and better hemodynamics, which is indirectly protective, but it does not mechanistically determine renal injury.
Re-intubation reflects respiratory failure and clinical deterioration often accompanied by hypotension, sepsis, or fluid overload, all of which increase AKI risk.
ICU visit is largely standard-of-care after cardiac surgery and provides little specific information about renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure in this cohort.
Low postoperative hemoglobin can reduce oxygen delivery to the kidney and correlates with bleeding/transfusion needs; anemia after cardiac surgery is an established AKI risk factor.
Postoperative hematocrit tracks anemia/hemodilution and bleeding severity, which relate to renal hypoxia and transfusion exposure, but is somewhat redundant with hemoglobin.
Remaining intubated postoperatively is a marker of higher acuity and often correlates with hemodynamic instability and fluid shifts that can contribute to AKI, though it is indirect.
Prior MI indicates underlying ischemic cardiomyopathy/coronary disease and higher risk of low cardiac output states postoperatively, which can impair renal perfusion.
Timing of prior MI may modestly stratify cardiac reserve and perioperative risk, but it is an indirect predictor of AKI compared with immediate postoperative hemodynamics and renal markers.
Symptom timing at admission reflects presentation history more than renal physiology; it has weak and nonspecific linkage to postoperative creatinine rise.
Symptom timing at surgery is primarily a scheduling/urgency proxy and is not a direct determinant of renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure.
Higher angina class reflects more severe coronary disease and potential for perioperative ischemia/low output, which can indirectly increase AKI risk, but it is not a direct renal predictor.
Higher NYHA class reflects worse baseline heart failure and lower cardiac reserve, increasing risk of perioperative low-output states and renal hypoperfusion.
Cardiogenic shock implies severe systemic hypoperfusion and renal ischemia, a direct and strong driver of postoperative AKI and creatinine rise.
Need for resuscitation suggests hemodynamic collapse, high vasopressor exposure, and ischemia-reperfusion injury, all strongly associated with AKI after cardiac surgery.
Arrhythmias can reduce effective cardiac output and renal perfusion, but as a broad category it is nonspecific and variably consequential for AKI.
Recent VT/VF indicates severe electrical instability often accompanied by hypotension/low flow and resuscitation, which are strong contributors to renal ischemic injury.
Sick sinus syndrome mainly reflects conduction disease and pacing need; it has only an indirect link to AKI unless associated with significant bradycardia/hypotension.
Atrial flutter can impair cardiac output and promote hemodynamic instability, but its AKI signal is generally weaker than shock or sustained hypotension.
Second-degree AV block may cause bradycardia and intermittent low output, but typically is treatable and not a primary established predictor of AKI.
Complete heart block can cause profound bradycardia and low cardiac output until paced, making renal hypoperfusion more likely perioperatively.
Being paced is a marker of conduction disease and management rather than a direct renal insult; association with AKI is indirect via underlying cardiac dysfunction.
Heart failure is an established risk factor for postoperative AKI due to reduced renal perfusion, venous congestion, and higher likelihood of low-output syndrome.
Recent atrial fibrillation is associated with perioperative hemodynamic instability and thromboembolic/low-output risk, moderately increasing AKI risk.
Preoperative pain score has no meaningful physiologic linkage to postoperative renal perfusion or tubular injury and is unlikely to predict creatinine rise.
Higher cell-saver volume can proxy surgical complexity/bleeding and transfusion exposure, which correlate with AKI risk, but it is an indirect marker.
Heparin dose largely reflects bypass protocol and patient size; it is not a known direct driver of AKI except in rare complications (e.g., HIT).
Protamine dose reflects heparin reversal and can be associated with hypotension or pulmonary vasoconstriction in reactions, indirectly affecting renal perfusion.
Viscoelastic testing (e.g., TEG/ROTEM) reflects bleeding/coagulopathy management rather than renal physiology. Any AKI link is indirect via transfusion/hemorrhagic shock risk.
Use of any volatile anesthetic is not a primary driver of postoperative AKI. It mainly proxies anesthetic technique and case complexity rather than renal perfusion injury.
Isoflurane has no strong established direct nephrotoxicity in typical cardiac anesthesia dosing. Any association with AKI would be confounded by hemodynamics and surgical complexity.

Sevoflurane has theoretical renal concerns (fluoride/compound A) but clinically significant AKI signal is weak in modern practice. At most an indirect marker of anesthetic choice and hemodynamic management.
Desflurane is not a recognized direct AKI risk factor. Any relationship would be indirect through blood pressure/vasopressor needs.
Other volatile agents are unlikely to be direct determinants of postoperative creatinine rise. This variable mostly captures practice variation rather than kidney injury mechanisms.
Pre-bypass volatile use is not a direct renal insult and is far from the 60h creatinine outcome. It is mainly a procedural/anesthetic workflow marker.
During-bypass anesthetic choice may correlate with bypass management and hemodynamic stability, which can affect renal perfusion. Still, volatile use itself is not a strong causal AKI predictor.
Post-bypass period is hemodynamically vulnerable, so anesthetic choice may weakly proxy hypotension/vasoplegia risk. The agent use itself is not a direct biomarker of AKI.
Maintenance volatile use reflects anesthetic strategy rather than renal injury. Any AKI association is likely confounded by illness severity and hemodynamic course.

Dexmedetomidine can influence sympathetic tone and blood pressure, potentially affecting renal perfusion, but evidence for strong AKI prediction is limited. More a marker of sedation strategy and patient stability.
Propofol infusion is not directly nephrotoxic and is common in cardiac anesthesia. AKI risk is mediated by hypotension/low flow states rather than propofol itself.
Midazolam is not a direct AKI risk factor; it mainly reflects sedation practice. Any link to creatinine rise would be indirect via hemodynamics or prolonged ventilation/illness severity.

Higher insulin requirements can proxy diabetes/stress hyperglycemia, which are associated with worse postoperative outcomes including AKI. However, insulin dose itself is not a direct renal injury mechanism.

Baseline systolic BP reflects chronic hypertension/vascular disease and starting perfusion pressure, both relevant to renal vulnerability during surgery. It is an established but nonspecific AKI risk indicator.

Diastolic BP relates to vascular tone and coronary/renal perfusion reserve and can reflect chronic hypertension. It is a plausible but less direct predictor than systolic pressure for postoperative AKI.

Pre-induction tachycardia can reflect pain/anxiety or limited cardiac reserve, which may correlate with perioperative instability. It is an indirect and nonspecific marker for renal hypoperfusion risk.

PAC use is mainly a proxy for higher surgical/heart failure complexity and clinician concern about hemodynamics. It is not a direct physiologic driver of AKI but may correlate with risk through severity of illness.

Peak core temperature intraoperatively is not a strong established predictor of post-cardiac surgery AKI compared with hypotension/low output and nephrotoxins. Hyperthermia may reflect inflammation/infection but is usually not the primary mechanism here.

Inhaled nitric oxide is typically used for pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction, which can be associated with low cardiac output and renal congestion. It is an indirect severity marker rather than a direct nephrotoxin.

Large crystalloid volumes can reflect bleeding/vasoplegia and contribute to fluid overload and renal venous congestion, both associated with AKI. However, volume given is confounded by case complexity and is not a direct biomarker.

Synthetic colloids (e.g., hydroxyethyl starch) have been associated with increased AKI risk in critical illness and can worsen renal outcomes. Even if use is limited in cardiac surgery, exposure is a plausible direct risk factor.

Albumin use is generally not nephrotoxic and often reflects attempts to support intravascular volume in vasoplegia/third spacing. It is more a marker of hemodynamic complexity than a direct AKI driver.

Perioperative dysglycemia is associated with worse outcomes and may reflect stress response and inflammation, which correlate with AKI risk. The trough value alone is indirect and less informative than sustained hyperglycemia or variability.

Intraoperative vasodilator use can indicate hypertension management or afterload reduction, but may also contribute to hypotension if over-titrated. Its relationship to AKI is indirect and highly confounded by hemodynamic context.

Intraoperative EEG monitoring is primarily for neurologic monitoring/anesthetic depth and does not directly relate to renal perfusion. It may weakly proxy case complexity but has minimal mechanistic link to AKI.
Pre-incision TEE use reflects higher-risk cardiac pathology or need for detailed hemodynamic assessment. It is a severity/complexity proxy rather than a direct predictor of renal injury.

Reduced LVEF is a well-established risk factor for low cardiac output syndrome and renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery. Baseline systolic dysfunction increases susceptibility to postoperative AKI.
RV dysfunction predisposes to elevated venous pressures, hepatic/renal congestion, and low forward flow, all of which increase AKI risk. It is a meaningful preoperative hemodynamic risk factor.

Significant MR can be associated with heart failure physiology and perioperative hemodynamic instability, indirectly increasing AKI risk. The link is weaker than direct measures of cardiac output or ventricular function.
MS can cause pulmonary hypertension and RV strain, which may contribute to renal congestion/low output perioperatively. It is an indirect risk factor and depends strongly on severity.

AR can reflect chronic volume overload and reduced effective forward perfusion during perioperative instability, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Its association with AKI is plausible but not as strong as ventricular dysfunction or shock markers.

Significant aortic valve disease can reduce forward flow and predispose to perioperative hypotension/low cardiac output, which increases AKI risk. It is an established but indirect predictor compared with direct hemodynamic/renal markers.
Whether aortic valve area was assessed is largely a documentation/measurement indicator rather than a physiologic exposure. It may weakly proxy case complexity but is not a direct AKI driver.
Tricuspid regurgitation can reflect right-sided dysfunction and venous congestion, which is associated with renal congestion and AKI after cardiac surgery. It is a moderate, indirect risk factor.
A patent foramen ovale has little direct relationship to renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Any association with AKI would be incidental via overall cardiac disease severity.
Assessment of the ascending aorta is primarily a procedural/documentation variable. It may correlate with surgical complexity but does not directly affect kidney injury risk.
A larger ascending aorta suggests aortopathy and potentially more complex surgery, which can increase AKI risk indirectly. It is not a direct physiologic determinant of renal injury.
Severe aortic atherosclerosis increases risk of embolic events and operative complexity, which can contribute to renal ischemia/inflammation. The link to AKI is plausible but indirect.
Mobile atheroma increases embolic potential and may reflect diffuse vascular disease, which can worsen postoperative organ perfusion. Association with AKI is plausible but not a primary driver.
Whether the arch was visualized is a technical/measurement detail without a direct mechanistic link to AKI. It mainly reflects imaging completeness.
Aortic arch atheroma indicates systemic atherosclerotic burden and higher embolic/complexity risk during surgery. This can indirectly increase AKI risk but is not a direct renal biomarker.
Mobile arch atheroma raises embolic risk and may correlate with perioperative hypoperfusion/inflammation. It is an indirect predictor of AKI rather than a direct cause.

Cardiopulmonary bypass is strongly associated with postoperative AKI via hemolysis, inflammation, nonpulsatile flow, and renal hypoperfusion. It is a major established exposure in cardiac-surgery AKI.
Autologous priming/retrograde autologous priming can reduce hemodilution and transfusion needs, potentially affecting AKI risk modestly. The effect is indirect and variable across practices.
Large crystalloid volumes during CPB contribute to hemodilution, tissue edema, and may reflect longer/complex bypass, all associated with AKI risk. It is a moderate, indirect predictor.

Synthetic colloids (especially hydroxyethyl starch, if used) have recognized nephrotoxic/AKI associations in critical illness and may worsen renal outcomes. Even when other colloids are used, higher colloid exposure can mark complex resuscitation.

Albumin is generally less nephrotoxic than synthetic colloids, but higher use may indicate significant volume resuscitation/vasoplegia and operative complexity. Its relationship to AKI is indirect and moderate at most.

Use of hemofiltration/perfusion around surgery suggests significant fluid overload, inflammatory load, or early renal support needs, all strongly associated with subsequent AKI/creatinine rise. It is not a direct creatinine biomarker but is a strong severity-of-illness/renal stress indicator.

Difficulty weaning from CPB requiring inotropes reflects low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk, a well-established AKI mechanism after cardiac surgery. It is indirect and can be confounded by transient myocardial stunning.

Need for vasopressors to wean from CPB indicates vasoplegia/shock physiology and impaired renal perfusion pressure, which strongly predicts postoperative AKI. It is a closer proxy for hemodynamic instability than many routine labs.
Having a post-TEE is mainly a marker of intraoperative complexity or concern rather than a direct renal mechanism. It may correlate with acuity but is not itself causative for AKI.
SAM can cause dynamic LVOT obstruction and hypotension, leading to renal hypoperfusion if sustained. It is relevant but typically episodic and treatable, so not a definitive AKI predictor alone.

Returning to CPB (or CPB-related echo prompting return) implies major intraoperative instability and longer bypass exposure, which increases AKI risk. However it is an indirect procedural marker and overlaps with other severity indicators.

Reduced LVEF is a known risk factor for postoperative AKI via low forward flow and venous congestion. Post-incision estimate captures baseline cardiac reserve but does not directly measure renal injury.

Postoperative RV dysfunction can drive high CVP/renal venous congestion and low effective renal perfusion, a strong pathway to AKI after cardiac surgery. It often correlates with difficult hemodynamics and prolonged recovery.
Intraoperative arrhythmias can cause hypotension and low cardiac output episodes, increasing renal ischemic risk. Association is moderate because many arrhythmias are brief and rapidly corrected.

Core temperature reflects hypothermia/rewarming and systemic stress but has only an indirect link to AKI compared with perfusion markers. Extremes may correlate with bypass duration, but temperature itself is not a primary driver.

Elevated INR may indicate hepatic hypoperfusion, hemodilution, or coagulopathy from CPB, which correlates with overall shock/bleeding risk and thus AKI indirectly. It is not a specific renal predictor.
Post-op leukocytosis is common from surgical stress and inflammation and is nonspecific for renal injury. It may weakly reflect systemic inflammatory response that can contribute to AKI.

Thrombocytopenia after CPB can reflect inflammation/consumption and is associated with worse postoperative outcomes including AKI, but it is indirect. It overlaps with bypass exposure and bleeding/transfusion effects.

Low hematocrit/anemia reduces oxygen delivery and often reflects bleeding/hemodilution/transfusion needs, which are established AKI risk factors after cardiac surgery. It is moderately predictive but not specific.

Fibrinogen changes reflect inflammation and coagulation status and may correlate with bleeding/transfusion, but links to AKI are indirect. It is less informative than hemodynamics or renal-specific labs.

Elevated lactate is a strong marker of global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output and predicts organ dysfunction including AKI after cardiac surgery. It captures severity and duration of shock physiology closely tied to renal ischemic injury.

Postoperative propofol exposure is not a direct nephrotoxin and usually reflects sedation strategy rather than renal physiology. Any AKI association is indirect via hypotension/vasopressor needs, which are better captured by hemodynamic features.
Pain score on POD3 is temporally distant and not mechanistically linked to creatinine rise at 60 hours. It mainly reflects comfort/analgesia practices rather than renal perfusion or injury.

Active smoking is associated with vascular disease and perioperative pulmonary complications, which can indirectly worsen renal perfusion. However it is not a strong short-term predictor of postoperative creatinine trajectory compared with hemodynamics and renal labs.

Former smoking is a weak proxy for chronic vascular risk but has limited incremental value for acute postoperative AKI prediction. Effects are indirect and often mediated by comorbidities captured elsewhere.

Paroxysmal AF can contribute to perioperative hemodynamic instability and need for rate/rhythm control, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Still, it is a modest predictor compared with direct markers like hypotension, low cardiac output, or renal labs.

Persistent AF more strongly reflects chronic atrial/structural heart disease and can predispose to low cardiac output and perioperative instability, increasing AKI risk. The link remains indirect but clinically established.

Antegrade cardioplegia delivery method is primarily a surgical technique variable and is not directly tied to renal injury. Any association with AKI would be confounded by procedure complexity and bypass time.

Retrograde cardioplegia may indicate more complex coronary anatomy or operative complexity, which can correlate with longer bypass/cross-clamp and AKI risk. It is still an indirect proxy rather than a direct renal insult.

Blood cardioplegia type has limited direct mechanistic linkage to postoperative renal dysfunction. Potential effects are small and largely mediated through overall operative course and perfusion management.

Crystalloid cardioplegia type is not a direct driver of AKI and mainly reflects institutional/surgeon preference. Any renal association would be indirect and likely weak compared with hemodynamic and renal biomarkers.

Acute heart failure around the time of surgery strongly increases risk of renal hypoperfusion and venous congestion, both key mechanisms for AKI. It is an established perioperative risk factor though not a direct biomarker.

Chronic heart failure predisposes to low renal perfusion reserve and susceptibility to perioperative hypotension and congestion. It is a well-known baseline risk factor but less specific than acute decompensation.

Systolic dysfunction is closely linked to low cardiac output states and need for inotropes/vasopressors, increasing AKI risk via renal hypoperfusion. It is an established predictor in cardiac surgery populations.

Diastolic dysfunction can cause elevated filling pressures and venous congestion, contributing to cardiorenal syndrome and AKI risk. The association is established but typically less direct than severe systolic failure.

Early mean oxygen saturation reflects global oxygen delivery but is often transiently affected by anesthesia/ventilation immediately post-op; per rules, 0–12h is capped conservatively. Hypoxemia can contribute to renal ischemic stress, but mean values may dilute brief insults.

Sustained lower oxygen saturation in the 13–24h trajectory phase suggests ongoing respiratory/circulatory compromise that can worsen renal oxygen delivery. As a mean, it is less sensitive than extremes but more informative than the immediate 0–12h period.

Sustained average oxygenation has only an indirect link to AKI via overall illness severity; renal hypoperfusion is more directly driven by hemodynamics than mean SpO2. Prefer extremes (min) for hypoxemic insult, so mean is capped to limit collinearity.
High SpO2 is not a trigger for AKI and mostly reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings. In the immediate post-op window it provides little prognostic information for creatinine rise.
Maximum SpO2 is not physiologically linked to kidney injury and is largely treatment-dependent. It adds minimal signal for AKI risk.
Maximum SpO2 is not a meaningful predictor of renal dysfunction and is heavily influenced by oxygen therapy. It is unlikely to add independent information beyond hypoxemia measures.

Hypoxemia can worsen renal medullary oxygen delivery and is a marker of cardiopulmonary instability, but early post-op desaturations are often transient. Capped by the 0–12h epoch maximum and conservatism.

Persistent or recurrent hypoxemia in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing respiratory/cardiac dysfunction and higher AKI risk via reduced oxygen delivery and severity of illness. Min captures the insult better than mean/max.

Late hypoxemic episodes close to the outcome window are a strong marker of ongoing instability and impaired oxygen delivery, which can contribute to AKI. The minimum value best captures clinically relevant hypoxemic insults.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine ICU practice rather than a renal mechanism. In the immediate post-op period it is nearly universal and not specific for impending AKI.
More frequent SpO2 documentation is a weak behavioral proxy for acuity but is nonspecific and common in ICU care. It has little direct relationship to creatinine rise.

Increased measurement frequency may reflect clinician concern for respiratory deterioration, which can correlate with overall severity and AKI risk, but remains indirect. As a behavioral proxy it is kept low.

Elevated mean PAP suggests RV strain/venous congestion that can impair renal perfusion, but early post-op values can be transient and treatment-influenced. Mean is less informative than extremes, so kept modest and within 0–12h cap.

Sustained pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction in this phase can contribute to renal venous congestion and low forward flow, increasing AKI risk. Mean is still less specific than max, limiting importance.

Persistently elevated mean PAP late post-op can indicate ongoing RV failure and systemic venous congestion, both associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. Mean captures sustained burden but is less specific than peak pressures.

Peak PAP early post-op can reflect acute RV afterload increase and hemodynamic instability that may reduce renal perfusion, but early derangements are common and transient. Scored at the 0–12h maximum allowed.

High peak PAP in the trajectory phase suggests significant RV strain/pulmonary hypertension with risk of low cardiac output and renal venous congestion. Max better captures clinically relevant hemodynamic insults than mean.

Late peak PAP elevations are a strong indicator of ongoing RV dysfunction and congestion close to the creatinine outcome window, which is strongly linked to AKI after cardiac surgery. The maximum value best captures acute hemodynamic stressors.

Low pulmonary artery pressure can reflect low RV output/low preload states that may reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op values are often transient and treatment-related. As a minimum of a mean signal, it is an indirect hemodynamic proxy rather than a direct AKI marker (and capped to avoid redundancy with more direct perfusion measures).

Persistently low PA pressures in the trajectory phase can indicate ongoing low cardiac output or underfilling, which increases AKI risk via reduced renal blood flow. Still indirect and influenced by ventilation/vasoactive management.

Closer to the 60h creatinine outcome, low PA pressures may better reflect sustained low forward flow or inadequate filling, both associated with renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Remains a surrogate compared with direct MAP/CI/urine output/creatinine trends.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity (e.g., presence of PA catheter) rather than renal physiology. Early post-op ordering is routine in higher-acuity cardiac surgery patients but is not specific for impending AKI.
More frequent PA pressure documentation suggests ongoing hemodynamic concern, but it is a behavioral proxy and not a mechanistic driver of AKI. Limited specificity for creatinine rise.
Continued frequent PA pressure measurements late may indicate persistent shock/heart failure physiology, which correlates with AKI risk. Still primarily a clinician-behavior marker, so kept low.

Frequency of continuous cardiac index recording reflects device use and acuity rather than kidney injury itself. In the immediate post-op period this is common and not specific for later creatinine rise.
Ongoing CI monitoring suggests hemodynamic instability, but measurement count is not a physiologic exposure. Weak, nonspecific association with AKI risk.
Late continued CI monitoring can signal persistent low-output or vasoactive dependence, which is associated with AKI. Still a proxy for concern rather than a direct predictor, so limited importance.

Higher mean CVP reflects venous congestion and elevated renal venous pressure, which can impair GFR, but early post-op CVP is heavily influenced by fluids, ventilation, and transient RV dysfunction. Mean is less sensitive than extremes for congestion-related AKI, so kept moderate and within the 0–12h cap.

Sustained elevated CVP in the trajectory phase is a recognized contributor to congestive nephropathy and AKI after cardiac surgery. Mean CVP over 12 hours can capture persistent congestion better than a single spike.

Persistently high CVP close to the outcome strongly suggests ongoing venous congestion/RV failure, a well-supported driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI. This window is a leading indicator for subsequent creatinine rise.

Peak CVP captures acute congestion episodes that can reduce renal perfusion gradient, but immediate post-op peaks are often transient (fluid boluses, PEEP changes). Extremes are more pathologic than means, but scoring is conservative due to early epoch cap.

High CVP maxima during 13–24h suggest significant congestion/RV dysfunction episodes, which are strongly linked to AKI via renal venous hypertension and reduced effective filtration pressure. More informative than mean for episodic decompensation.

Very high CVP close to 60h is a strong direct predictor of AKI in cardiac surgery patients, reflecting ongoing venous congestion and impaired renal perfusion gradient. Captures severe episodes that are particularly nephrotoxic.

Low CVP minima may indicate hypovolemia/underfilling and potential renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op lows are common and rapidly corrected. Less specific for AKI than high CVP (congestion) in this population.

Low CVP can reflect relative hypovolemia and reduced renal perfusion, but the minimum is less informative than sustained elevation for renal venous congestion and is also sensitive to transient measurement/positioning effects.

In the closer pre-outcome window, a low CVP minimum may indicate inadequate preload/volume status contributing to renal hypoperfusion, though it is still less directly linked to AKI than high CVP/venous congestion metrics.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism; early post-op CVP checks are routine after cardiac surgery.
More frequent CVP documentation suggests clinician concern or instability but is not a direct physiologic driver of AKI.
Increased CVP measurement frequency late may proxy ongoing hemodynamic management/concern (e.g., shock, RV failure), which can correlate with AKI risk, but remains indirect.
Higher average FiO2 reflects respiratory failure/illness severity that can co-occur with AKI, but early post-op oxygen needs are common and nonspecific.
Persistently elevated FiO2 in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing pulmonary dysfunction and higher overall severity, indirectly increasing AKI risk via hypoxemia/inflammation/hemodynamic stress.
Sustained higher FiO2 close to hour 60 indicates persistent critical illness and possible hypoxemia/ARDS, which is moderately associated with subsequent AKI.
FiO2 spikes can mark acute hypoxemia or instability, but in the immediate post-op period transient high FiO2 is common; max is preferred over mean for capturing acute insult.
A high FiO2 maximum during 13–24h suggests episodic hypoxemia or deterioration, which can contribute to renal ischemic risk indirectly; max captures the insult better than mean.

Late high FiO2 peaks indicate significant respiratory compromise near the outcome window and correlate with systemic hypoxia/vasopressor needs, increasing AKI risk (still indirect vs renal-specific markers).
Minimum FiO2 largely reflects weaning/ventilator practice and is not a strong mechanistic predictor of AKI; early post-op minima are especially protocol-driven.
Lower minimum FiO2 generally indicates improvement and is weakly informative for AKI risk compared with hypoxemic extremes.
In the late window, inability to reduce FiO2 (higher minimum) can indicate persistent oxygenation failure and higher illness severity, but it remains an indirect AKI predictor.
FiO2 documentation frequency is routine in ventilated post-cardiac surgery patients and mainly reflects charting/monitoring rather than AKI physiology.
Measurement frequency is a behavioral proxy and provides little incremental physiologic information for AKI prediction.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician attention/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Higher FiO2 monitoring may correlate with respiratory failure/shock, but is an indirect proxy only.

Ventilator rate settings reflect respiratory management and acid-base goals; links to AKI are indirect (severity of illness, CO2/acid-base effects on renal perfusion). In the immediate 0–12h post-op period this is often transient and protocol-driven.

Persistently higher set rates may indicate ongoing respiratory failure/metabolic acidosis and higher illness severity, which can track with AKI risk. Mean rate is still a smoothed, management-driven variable rather than a direct renal insult.

A high maximum set rate can mark episodes of acidosis/ventilatory difficulty and higher physiologic stress, indirectly associated with AKI. Early post-op extremes are common and often reflect short-lived management changes (cap at 6 for 0–12h).

A higher max set rate in 13–24h suggests persistent/worsening respiratory or metabolic derangement and greater severity of illness, which is more predictive of downstream AKI than early transient changes. Max captures acute escalation better than mean (avoid redundancy with mean).
Low minimum set rate usually reflects sedation/weaning strategy rather than a renal-perfusion insult. In 0–12h it is heavily protocolized and weakly related to AKI.
Lower minimum set rate generally indicates stability or ventilator weaning and has no direct mechanistic link to AKI. Any association would be confounding by overall recovery trajectory.
Measurement frequency is a documentation/monitoring artifact and not a physiologic driver of AKI. In early post-op care, ventilator settings are routinely charted regardless of renal risk.
Charting frequency reflects workflow and attention rather than kidney injury biology. It is unlikely to add specific prognostic value for creatinine rise.
More frequent documentation may reflect instability or active ventilator titration, indirectly correlating with severity of illness and AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, so kept low.

Higher tidal volumes can contribute to ventilator-induced lung injury and systemic inflammation, which may indirectly worsen AKI risk, but settings are often protocolized early post-op. Mean values smooth short episodes and are not direct renal markers.

Sustained higher tidal volumes may indicate ongoing ventilatory needs and potential inflammatory burden, indirectly associated with AKI. Mean remains management-driven and less specific than extremes.

In the 25–36h window, persistently higher tidal volume settings can reflect ongoing respiratory failure and systemic stress/inflammation that track with AKI risk. Still indirect and influenced by ventilator strategy rather than kidney physiology.

A high maximum tidal volume may capture brief periods of non-protective ventilation or ventilatory difficulty, indirectly linked to systemic inflammation and AKI. Early post-op changes are often transient and protocol-related (cap at 6 for 0–12h).

Higher max tidal volume in 13–24h suggests ongoing need for aggressive ventilation or poor lung mechanics, correlating with severity of illness and AKI risk. Max better captures potentially injurious episodes than mean (avoid collinearity).

A high max tidal volume close to the outcome window may reflect persistent respiratory failure or episodes of non-protective ventilation, contributing to systemic inflammation and hemodynamic effects that can worsen renal perfusion. It is still an indirect predictor rather than a renal biomarker.

Low set tidal volume is usually a lung-protective strategy and only indirectly relates to AKI via severity of respiratory failure/ventilation needs. In the immediate 0–12h post-op period it is often protocol-driven and transient.
Minimum set tidal volume mainly reflects ventilator strategy rather than renal perfusion; any AKI link is indirect through illness severity. Not a primary driver of creatinine rise.

Persistently very low set tidal volumes by 25–36h may indicate ongoing ARDS/severe respiratory failure, which can correlate with shock/inflammation and AKI risk. Still an indirect marker rather than a direct renal insult.
Measurement frequency is a documentation/monitoring artifact and not a physiologic mechanism for AKI. Ventilator settings are routinely charted in the early post-op period.
Frequency of recording tidal volume settings reflects workflow and monitoring intensity more than renal risk. Not specific for impending AKI.
More frequent ventilator setting documentation can weakly proxy higher acuity or instability, which may co-occur with AKI. Still largely behavioral and nonspecific.

Higher mean PEEP can reduce venous return/cardiac output and raise renal venous pressure, indirectly worsening renal perfusion. In 0–12h it is often transient and protocolized, so predictive value is limited.
Sustained higher PEEP during 13–24h may reflect ongoing hypoxemia and can contribute to reduced renal perfusion via hemodynamic effects. Still indirect and less informative than extremes.

Persistently elevated mean PEEP close to the outcome window can indicate ongoing respiratory failure and sustained hemodynamic compromise affecting kidney perfusion. Mean is less specific than max for acute insults, but sustained exposure can matter.

High peak PEEP can acutely impair preload and renal blood flow, a plausible contributor to AKI risk. In the immediate post-op window, brief high PEEP is common (recruitment) so cap at moderate importance.

A high PEEP maximum in 13–24h suggests significant hypoxemia/ARDS management and potential hemodynamic compromise, both associated with AKI. Extremes better capture acute renal perfusion threats than means.

High maximum PEEP in 25–36h is a strong marker of ongoing severe lung disease and potential right-heart/venous congestion or low cardiac output, which can directly worsen renal perfusion. As an extreme close to hour 60, it is a stronger leading indicator.
Minimum PEEP often reflects baseline ventilator settings (e.g., 5 cmH2O) and is not a known trigger for AKI. In 0–12h it is especially protocol-driven.
Low minimum PEEP is generally not harmful to renal perfusion and is weakly related to AKI risk. It adds little beyond max/mean PEEP.
A very low minimum PEEP could reflect readiness to wean and lower illness severity (inverse association), but it is nonspecific. Less informative than PEEP max for renal risk.
PEEP measurement frequency is primarily a charting/monitoring behavior and not a physiologic determinant of AKI. Early post-op ventilator parameters are routinely recorded.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician attention/vent management rather than a direct renal perfusion mechanism. Could weakly proxy severity of respiratory failure/PEEP titration affecting venous return.
Ordering/recording frequency is a behavioral acuity marker, not a physiologic driver of AKI. May weakly correlate with ongoing instability requiring ventilator adjustments.

Higher sustained plateau pressures can reflect poor lung compliance and higher intrathoracic pressures that may reduce venous return/cardiac output, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. In the immediate post-op window this is often transient and less specific for AKI.

Persistently elevated plateau pressure suggests ongoing lung injury/ARDS physiology and higher intrathoracic pressures that can impair renal perfusion indirectly. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute insult, so kept modest to reduce collinearity.

Peak plateau pressure captures episodes of high intrathoracic pressure that can acutely reduce cardiac output and renal blood flow. Early post-op derangements are common, so capped by the 0–12h conservatism rule.

High plateau pressure spikes in the trajectory phase can indicate evolving lung injury and hemodynamic compromise (reduced preload/CO), which is a plausible contributor to AKI risk. Extremes are more pathophysiologically relevant than means.
Low minimum plateau pressure is generally not a trigger for AKI and may simply reflect periods of lower support or better compliance. Provides limited renal-specific prognostic information.
Minimum plateau pressure is not a recognized driver of renal injury and is less informative than high extremes for hemodynamic impact. Likely redundant with other ventilatory severity markers.
Frequency of plateau pressure measurement reflects clinician concern/ventilator management rather than kidney physiology. In early post-op care it is often routine and nonspecific.
Measurement frequency is a weak proxy for respiratory severity and monitoring intensity, not a direct AKI mechanism. Could correlate with overall acuity but remains low-importance.
More frequent plateau checks late may indicate difficult ventilation/ARDS and higher overall illness severity, which can track with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, so kept within the 1–3 range.

Higher minute ventilation can reflect metabolic acidosis, sepsis, or increased dead space, indirectly associated with AKI risk. In the immediate post-op period it is heavily influenced by ventilator settings and transient physiology.

Sustained elevated minute ventilation may signal ongoing acidosis/critical illness burden that correlates with renal injury risk. Mean is less specific than extremes for acute deterioration, so moderate score.

Spikes in minute ventilation can indicate acute acidosis, agitation, or worsening gas exchange, which may accompany hemodynamic stress and AKI risk. Early post-op variability is common, limiting specificity.

High minute ventilation peaks during 13–24h can reflect worsening metabolic acidosis or respiratory failure, both markers of systemic stress linked to AKI. Extremes better capture deterioration than averages.
Low minimum minute ventilation is usually due to ventilator weaning/sedation changes and is not a typical AKI trigger. Limited direct linkage to renal perfusion or tubular injury.

Low minute ventilation can reflect reduced respiratory drive/sedation or ventilator weaning rather than renal physiology; only indirectly relates to AKI via overall illness severity and acid–base status. As a minimum (extreme), it may capture brief hypoventilation but is not a primary AKI driver.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a causal pathway to AKI. Early post-op (0–12h) frequent vent documentation is routine in cardiac ICU.
Represents clinician monitoring/vent management intensity, not kidney perfusion or nephrotoxicity. May weakly proxy ongoing instability but remains indirect.

More frequent minute ventilation measurements at 25–36h can indicate persistent respiratory failure/critical illness, which correlates with multi-organ dysfunction including AKI. Still a behavioral proxy, so capped low.

Average oxygen device level in the immediate post-op window is heavily influenced by routine ventilation/oxygen protocols and transient effects of anesthesia/CPB. Only indirectly associated with AKI through overall severity/hypoxemia.

Sustained need for higher oxygen support may reflect ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction and systemic illness that can predispose to AKI. Mean aggregation blunts acute hypoxemic episodes, limiting specificity.

Persistent higher oxygen device requirement closer to hour 60 suggests unresolved respiratory/cardiac dysfunction and higher risk of multi-organ failure including AKI. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute insults, so kept moderate.

Maximum oxygen device requirement may capture acute respiratory failure/hypoxemia, which can worsen renal oxygen delivery and correlate with shock severity. In 0–12h this is often expected post-op, so conservatively scored.

A high maximum oxygen device requirement in 13–24h suggests significant respiratory compromise or pulmonary edema, often accompanying low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Extremes better capture clinically meaningful deterioration than means.

High peak oxygen device requirement at 25–36h is a strong marker of ongoing respiratory failure/possible cardiogenic pulmonary edema and overall severity, which is associated with subsequent AKI. Still indirect (not a renal biomarker), so not higher.
Minimum oxygen device requirement mainly reflects periods of stability or weaning and has no direct mechanistic link to AKI. In the immediate post-op period it is particularly non-informative.
A low minimum oxygen device level indicates transient stability and does not predict renal injury. It is not an AKI trigger and adds little beyond other severity markers.
Minimum oxygen device requirement at 25–36h reflects best observed respiratory status rather than hypoxemic burden. It is unlikely to add predictive value for creatinine rise.
Frequency of documenting oxygen device is a care-process/acuity proxy rather than a physiologic determinant of AKI. In 0–12h it is routine and weakly informative.
More frequent oxygen device entries may indicate closer monitoring for respiratory instability, indirectly correlating with illness severity. As a measured feature, it is capped low.

At 25–36h, frequent oxygen device reassessment can signal persistent respiratory failure or escalation/weaning attempts, which correlates with higher AKI risk via multi-organ dysfunction. Still a behavioral proxy, so limited to 1–3.

Higher average chest tube output can reflect bleeding/transfusion needs and hemodynamic stress that may contribute to AKI, but the mean is less informative than peak bleeding. Early (0–12h) outputs are often transient post-op.

Persistently elevated average drainage may indicate ongoing bleeding/inflammation with potential renal hypoperfusion, but mean remains a smoothed proxy and less specific than extremes. Trajectory phase adds some prognostic value.

Ongoing average chest tube output this late suggests continued bleeding or coagulopathy with potential volume depletion/transfusion exposure, both linked to AKI risk. Still less specific than maximum output for acute insult.
Peak early chest tube output captures acute bleeding risk that can drive hypotension, transfusion, and renal hypoperfusion—important AKI mechanisms. Capped by 0–12h epoch maximum score.

A high maximum output in 13–24h suggests ongoing/secondary bleeding or tamponade physiology risk, which can impair cardiac output and renal perfusion. Extremes better capture the pathologic insult than means.

Late high peak chest tube output is abnormal and may indicate significant ongoing bleeding or hemodynamic compromise close to the outcome window, strongly increasing AKI risk via hypoperfusion/transfusion burden. Max captures the acute insult best.

Minimum chest tube output is generally not a direct AKI driver and may reflect routine low drainage rather than pathology. Low output could be concerning for obstruction/tamponade only in context, so it is weak alone.

Minimum output remains nonspecific for renal outcomes and is less informative than maximum output for bleeding-related hypoperfusion. Potential tamponade signal is indirect without concurrent hemodynamics.

Very low output late could rarely suggest tube occlusion with risk of retained blood/tamponade and reduced cardiac output, indirectly affecting kidneys. Still an indirect and context-dependent marker compared with max output.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects nursing/ICU routine documentation rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI. Early post-op monitoring is standard and not specific for impending renal injury.
How often output is recorded is a behavioral/monitoring proxy and typically protocolized, offering little specific signal for AKI risk. Not a direct physiologic predictor.
More frequent measurement late may reflect concern for bleeding or instability, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it remains an indirect behavioral proxy. Kept low per the _measured rule.

Elevated mean PAP can indicate RV strain/pulmonary hypertension and lower effective cardiac output, indirectly reducing renal perfusion. In 0–12h this is often transient post-bypass/ventilation effects, so conservative scoring.

Sustained higher mean PAP in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing RV dysfunction or high PVR, which can impair forward flow and renal perfusion. Mean is less sensitive than extremes but still clinically relevant.

Persistently elevated mean PAP close to hour 60 can reflect ongoing RV failure/venous congestion, both associated with cardiorenal syndrome and AKI. Mean captures sustained congestion burden rather than brief spikes.
Peak PAP early may capture acute RV afterload crises (hypoxia, hypercarbia, pulmonary vasoconstriction) that can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. Capped by the 0–12h epoch maximum score.

Elevated PA systolic pressure can reflect RV strain/venous congestion that may contribute to renal dysfunction, but it is indirect and often confounded by ventilation and volume status. Max is the most relevant aggregation, but in this mid window it is only a moderate signal.

Persistently high PA systolic pressure closer to the outcome can indicate ongoing RV failure and renal venous congestion, which is associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. Still an indirect marker compared with MAP/urine output/creatinine.

Low PA systolic pressure may indicate low pulmonary flow/low cardiac output, which can reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op values are highly transient and influenced by anesthesia/ventilation. Minimum is relevant for shock physiology but capped by early epoch conservatism.

A low PA systolic minimum in this trajectory phase can reflect episodes of low cardiac output that threaten renal perfusion. It remains indirect and less specific than systemic hypotension or cardiac index.
Late low PA systolic episodes may signal ongoing low output state, a recognized driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI. Minimum captures acute hypoperfusion better than mean.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Early post-op PA monitoring is common after cardiac surgery, limiting specificity for AKI risk.
More frequent PA pressure documentation may indicate hemodynamic instability prompting closer monitoring, but it is a behavioral proxy and nonspecific. Therefore kept low.

Continued frequent PA pressure measurements late may reflect persistent shock/RV failure concern, which correlates with AKI risk, but it remains an indirect clinician-behavior marker. Scored at the upper end allowed for _measured.

Mean PA diastolic pressure can correlate with left-sided filling pressures and pulmonary vascular tone, indirectly relating to congestion and renal venous pressure. Mean smooths extremes and early post-op values are often transient.

Sustained higher PA diastolic pressure may suggest elevated filling pressures and congestion, which can worsen renal function via venous hypertension. Mean is less informative than max/min for acute insults, so kept moderate.

Later sustained elevation in PA diastolic pressure can indicate ongoing congestion/left-sided pressures, a plausible contributor to AKI through renal venous congestion and reduced net filtration pressure. Still indirect compared with direct renal markers.
